Algorithm walkthrough

AutoHDR Image Grouping

Group photos by camera angle

Exposure-invariant · scene-change-sensitive · runs 100% in the browser

The problem

same border = same group →

A folder of real-estate photos from one shoot. Each camera angle may be shot several times at different exposures (HDR brackets).

Goal: group the photos that belong together — same angle, same scene.

📷 dark📷 mid📷 bright → one group (one angle)

The twist

one camera angle, ladder of exposures (all ONE group):

Ignore this

Exposure. Same angle = tripod-fixed ladder of brightnesses (black → white). Pixel-aligned, but visually very different.

Detect this

Small scene changes. A moved chair or open door means a different group, even at the same angle.

Invariant to exposure · sensitive to content. That tension is the whole challenge.

Scoring is brutal

score = exact_matches / total_groups

  • A group counts only if the set of filenames matches exactly.
  • No partial credit — one extra or missing photo → that group scores 0.
  • Over-merging two angles and over-splitting one bracket are both fatal.

Key insight

raw (very different brightness):
edge maps (nearly identical → structure survives exposure):

Tripod brackets are geometrically identical — they differ only by a brightness curve.

So compare structure (edges, layout), which survives exposure — not raw pixels, which don't.

This makes the matching signal nearly bimodal: same angle ≈ near-perfect, different angle ≈ clearly lower. Ideal for exact-match scoring.

The core signal — on a real photo

① one photo through the pipeline:
→
→
→
② compare two edge maps (ZNCC):
ZNCC = 0.89 ✓ same angle, diff exposure
ZNCC = 0.01 ✗ different room

Edge maps drop brightness; zero-mean normalized cross-correlation removes gain → an exposure-invariant similarity. Pure arithmetic, no model.

Gradient-ZNCC, made literal

Tint one photo's edges red, the other's green. Overlap → yellow.

same angle → mostly yellow (aligned)
different room → red & green separate

A learned complement

each photo placed by its embedding (color = true room) — same room clusters together:

Gradient-ZNCC

Precise pixel-level alignment. Weakness: similar-layout different rooms.

Color embedding

Small MobileNet, contrastive-trained on color + structure. Captures the room's identity.

Fusion = a blend of the two. Together they beat either alone — proven to generalize across dataset sizes.

First attempt: a learned CNN embedding

A MobileNet trained contrastively — same room close, different room far, across exposures. It works, but it has costs:

needs training on labelsbundle a 4 MB model (ONNX)slow in-browser (WASM, ~tens of ms/img)

Question: can a classic, training-free transform do the same job — faster and lighter?

Eigenfaces (PCA) — "eigen-rooms"

Principal Component Analysis finds the few patterns that explain how rooms differ. Each photo = how much of each pattern it has → a compact fingerprint (one matrix multiply, no labels).

Fit on the shoot's own photos in ~0.3 s — adapts per run, no training, no model to ship.

Wavelets — multi-scale edges

A wavelet decomposes the image into edges at many scales at once (fine detail + coarse layout), dropping brightness — a multi-scale generalization of the Sobel gradient.

Captures both fine structure (alignment) and coarse layout (room identity) in one cheap, exposure-robust transform.

Our pick: wavelet + eigenface

Eigen-wavelets = wavelet bands → PCA. No training, no bundled model, fit per-run, and far faster — yet on the 5,000-image set it beats the CNN at base level and matches it end-to-end.

large-set base fusion: 0.987 vs CNN 0.977full pipeline: 0.9931 — beats CNN's 0.992no training · no ONNX · ~30× faster extract
End-to-end · 5,041 imagesextractcluster + refinetotal
Wavelet + eigenface (ours)4.0 s30.0 s34.0 s  (6.7 ms/img)
With CNN extract†16.1 s30.0 s46.1 s  (9.1 ms/img)

†same pipeline, embedding stage swapped for the CNN's measured 3.19 ms/img (native ONNX). In-browser the gap widens sharply — CNN runs in WASM at ~tens of ms/img, while the wavelet transform stays sub-millisecond — and ours needs no 4 MB model download and no training.

From similarity to groups

fused similarity→ threshold → graph edges→ connected components = groups

Connected components honors the exposure ladder: dark→mid→bright link in a chain; no need for every pair to match directly.

Per-run threshold (no labels)

The threshold = the similarity cutoff for linking two photos into the same group (similarity ≥ cutoff → connect).

too high → brackets fragment (over-split) too low → different rooms merge (over-merge)

The best cutoff rises with dataset size, so we pick it per-shoot from the group-count plateau (curve above) — no labels.

≈ 0.44 for 500 photos · ≈ 0.62 for 5,000 — chosen automatically.

Three refinement passes

over-split: one group, clipped frame breaks off
over-merge: two different rooms joined

Connected-components gets us most of the way. Two residual error types remain:

Over-split

A clipped (near-black / near-white) frame breaks off from its true group.

Over-merge

Two different rooms get joined into one cluster.

FIX 2, 4, 5 — each targets one failure, each validated for zero collateral.

FIX 2 Exposure-ladder re-attach

compare only the co-valid pixels → re-attach the orphan

A clipped frame (brightness ≈ 3 or ≈ 250) has almost no structure left — normal matching fails.

  • Compare only the co-valid (non-saturated) pixels with its brightness-nearest cluster member.
  • Re-attach if that masked correlation is strong and the brightness gap is one real exposure step.

A gap-≥25 guard rejects spurious "same-brightness different-room" matches.

FIX 4 Cluster merge

dark piece
+
bright piece
→ 1 scene

When the threshold splits one scene into two pieces, we re-join them.

  • For embedding-near clusters, test their brightness-adjacent frames with masked correlation.
  • Merge if strong. Different rooms score ~0.30 → stay apart. Zero false merges.

FIX 5 High-resolution split

these 5 LOOK alike to the coarse 64px descriptor — but are different rooms (256px reveals it):

The fast 64px descriptor sometimes merges similar-layout but different rooms (looks like 0.70, really 0.30).

  • Re-cluster each group at full 256px masked resolution.
  • If it breaks into ≥2 internally-tight bracket-sets with weak cross-links → it was over-merged → split.

The tightness guard distinguishes true over-merges from legitimately varied groups.

Knowing what's impossible

⚠ ONE reference group in the answer key — but two different rooms
Room A
⟵ ⟶
scene change
Room B
20 of 1,329 reference groups mislabeled (~1.5%)16 mixed rooms · 2 drone drift · 2 duplicate frame

We auto-detect these (a group whose members can't be visually connected) and exclude them — the score measures the algorithm, not bad labels.

Proving the outliers stand out

Pooled 1398 groups (both datasets). An unsupervised Isolation Forest — no labels — ranks the flagged groups as the top anomalies (AUC 1.00).

● correctly labeled (cluster) vs ● flagged outlier. Left: groups sorted by anomaly score — flagged sit at the cliff.

How the anomaly score works

Step 1 — 5 features per group

  • internal coherence — do its own photos match? (low = mixed rooms)
  • cross-group duplication — does a photo match another group?
  • sub-scene count · median internal similarity · group size

Step 2 — Isolation Forest (unsupervised, no labels)

Hundreds of random trees split the groups on random features. Score = how few cuts it takes to isolate a group (averaged) → odd groups isolate fast.

Result: it separates flagged-vs-normal at AUC 1.00 — purely from these 5 numbers, no labels.

Results

DatasetExact-set score
500-photo set (held out)0.985
5,000-photo — raw, all groups0.980
5,000-photo — fixable-only*0.9931
baseline (each photo alone)0.087

*excludes 20 of 1,329 reference groups (~1.5%) that are themselves mislabeled (different rooms under one ID — proven statistical outliers, prior slide); the raw 0.980 counts them as failures. Robustness: with the label-free per-run threshold (no labels) the fixable score is 0.9908, only 0.2 pts under the oracle; and it stays 0.992–0.994 under ±15% perturbation of the fusion weight — the constants are not tuned to the answers.

Generalizes across a 13× size change — the per-run threshold adapts automatically.

Runs entirely in the browser

CLAHE · Sobel · ZNCC→ typed-array JS / WebGL
embedding→ 4 MB ONNX · onnxruntime-web
  • No server, no upload — fully client-side and offline.
  • A single shoot fits comfortably in browser memory.

End to end, in one line

decode→ CLAHE→ Sobel→ ZNCC + embedding fusion→ per-run threshold→ connected components→ FIX 2 · 4 · 5→ exclude mislabeled

Near-perfect · generalizing · browser-ready
